dna-reader.readerDescription: Educated general reader, 35–60, curious about how social systems form and fail. Reads longform journalism and popular history. Interested in understanding present-day dynamics through their historical roots. Not an academic.
dna-reader.readerProblem: Wants to understand why communities fracture along lines that seem arbitrary — and whether those fractures are inevitable

dna-transform.beforeState: Sees community collapse as random or driven by bad actors
dna-transform.afterState: Understands the specific structural conditions that make communities vulnerable, and what the historical record says about prevention

dna-idea.bigIdea: Communities don't fracture because of bad actors — they fracture because of broken information flows that make collective sensemaking impossible.

dna-author.authorAngle: Investigative journalist who spent 18 months embedded in three communities during periods of acute social fracture. First-hand reporting combined with historical analysis.

dna-promise.corePromise: A historically grounded account of why communities fracture — and what the evidence says about prevention.
dna-promise.workingSubtitle: Information, Trust, and the Hidden Conditions of Community Collapse

comps.title: Bowling Alone
comps.author: Robert Putnam
comps.whatItDoes: Definitive account of declining social capital

comps.title: The Enigma of Reason
comps.author: Mercier & Sperber
comps.whatItDoes: Why humans reason in groups, not individually
comps.gap: Cognitive science framing; no historical case studies

dna-comps.gap: First-hand reported account of community fracture from inside, combined with historical pattern analysis. Neither Putnam nor Mercier has the narrative texture.

dna-evidence.uncertaintyHandling: First-person sources named where possible; anonymised where necessary. Clear distinction between what was observed and what documents say.

dna-commercial.successMetric: 15,000 sales in 12 months; review coverage in quality press

dna-consolidate.positioningStatement: For general readers curious about social fracture and its historical roots, The Silence That Built Us combines first-hand reporting from inside three fracturing communities with historical analysis to argue that community collapse follows a predictable information-failure pattern — and is therefore preventable.

pb-thesis.centralQuestion: Why do communities fracture — and does the historical record reveal any conditions that make fracture preventable?
pb-thesis.thesisAnswer: Community collapse follows a predictable sequence: first, information flows break down; then, collective sensemaking fails; then, trust collapses faster than institutions can repair it. The sequence is not random, and it is interrupcible.

pb-timeline.pivotMoment: 2021 — Site 1 community meeting where information was deliberately withheld by institutional actors

timelineEvents.description: Site 1 founding; community information systems established
Site 2 fracture begins — council withholds budget information
timelineEvents.significance: First documented information failure in sequence

timelineEvents.description: Site 1 fracture trigger — information withheld at town hall
timelineEvents.significance: Pivot moment — marks start of collapse sequence

timelineEvents.description: Site 3 successful repair — transparency mechanisms restored trust

pb-fork.rationale: Three sites are used as evidence for an argument about information failure — the argument organises the structure, not the chronology of events.
